title: Java Stream Lazy Pipelines
description: Practice why stream pipelines stay lazy and can be consumed only once.
learningGoals: Recognize that intermediate operations are lazy and run only when a terminal operation executes.
Explain why a consumed stream throws IllegalStateException on a second terminal operation.

completionChecks.explanation: filter drops Ada because its length is 3, map upper-cases the survivors, and toList preserves the encounter order of the source list.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens if pipeline.count() runs after the toList() call?
choices: It returns 3 because the pipeline re-reads the source list
It returns 0 because the stream is now empty
It throws IllegalStateException because the stream has already been operated upon
It fails to compile because count() does not exist on Stream<String>
completionChecks.explanation: A stream can be traversed only once; after toList() consumes the pipeline, any further intermediate or terminal call throws IllegalStateException.

completionChecks.prompt: What does the code print if the toList and list print are replaced with System.out.println(pipeline.count())?
choices: It throws IllegalStateException because count is a terminal operation
completionChecks.explanation: The trap is expecting the map result to print; count is the first terminal operation and counts the three names that pass the lazy filter.
